coredata.dc:title: Double Z-scheme photocatalyst C3N4 nanotube/N-doped carbon dots/Ni2P with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yingying
Li, Yike
Wang, Jianshe
He, Zhanhang
Li, Zhongjun
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalyst with double Z-scheme mechanism can promote the separation and transmission of photo-induced carriers effectively. In this work, a novelC3N4 nanotube (CNT)/N-doped carbon dots (NCDs)/Ni2P composite photocatalyst was prepared by decorating NCDs and Ni2P nanoparticles on CNT, respectively. A variety of test results, including photoluminescence spectra (PL), electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses (TPR), show that compared with CNT and CNT/Ni2P, CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite can dramatically facilitate the separation and transmission of charge. Impressively, the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite achieves a hydrogen evolution rate of 627.2 µmol g-1h−1 which is well above that of CNT and CNT/Ni2P, respectively (no detection and 48.5 μmol g-1h−1). Combining the above characterization results with energy band calculation, we speculate that the remarkably optimized photocatalytic activity was predominantly ascribed to the double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanismfor CNT/NCDs/Ni2P nanocomposite. This work provides a new member for family of photocatalysts with double Z-scheme mechanism, which is beneficial to further fundamental and applied research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride nanotube
N-doped carbon dots
Ni2P
Double Z-scheme heterojunction
Hydrogen production

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of bulk C3N4, CNT, CNT/Ni2P-0.5% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%.
SEM images of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% (a) lateral view and (b) cross-section view; (c) TEM image of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%; (d) HRTEM image of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%.
The XPS spectra of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composite: (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) Ni 2p, and (d) P 2p.
UV–vis DRS spectra of bulk C3N4, C3N4 nanotube, CNT/Ni2P and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites.
(a) FTIR spectra of bulk C3N4 and typical CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%; (b) FTIR spectra of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites with different Ni2P amounts.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption desorption curve and (b) corresponding pore size distributions of bulk C3N4 and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% complexes.

(a) The rate of H2 evolution over CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% photocatalysts loaded with 0.5% Ni2P and different amounts of NCDs (λ ≥ 420 nm). (b) Photocatalytic reaction of different photocatalyst in visible Light (λ ≥ 420 nm) for 3 h. (A: bulk C3N4 B: CNT/NCDs. C: CNT/Ni2P-0.5%. D: CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.2%. E: CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%. F: CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.0%. G: CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.5%).

(a) Photocatalytic hydrogen production over (CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%) during 15 h, with evacuation every 3 h; (b) The PL spectra of the as-prepared composites; (c) I-t curves and (d) EIS spectra of bulk C3N4, CNT/Ni2P-0.5% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% under visible light (>420 nm) in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte.
(a) Estimated band gaps of bulk C3N4 and CNT samples and (b) Mott-Schottky plots of CNT.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for hydrogen evolution over CNT/NCDs/Ni2P under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Double Z-scheme photocatalyst C3N4 nanotube/N-doped carbon dots/Ni2P with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalyst with double Z-scheme mechanism can promote the separation and transmission of photo-induced carriers effectively. In this work, a novelC3N4 nanotube (CNT)/N-doped carbon dots (NCDs)/Ni2P composite photocatalyst was prepared by decorating NCDs and Ni2P nanoparticles on CNT, respectively. A variety of test results, including photoluminescence spectra (PL), electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses (TPR), show that compared with CNT and CNT/Ni2P, CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite can dramatically facilitate the separation and transmission of charge. Impressively, the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite achieves a hydrogen evolution rate of 627.2 µmol g-1h−1 which is well above that of CNT and CNT/Ni2P, respectively (no detection and 48.5 μmol g-1h−1). Combining the above characterization results with energy band calculation, we speculate that the remarkably optimized photocatalytic activity was predominantly ascribed to the double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanismfor CNT/NCDs/Ni2P nanocomposite. This work provides a new member for family of photocatalysts with double Z-scheme mechanism, which is beneficial to further fundamental and applied research.

ce:section.ce:para: As a metal-free organic semiconductor, polymeric carbon nitride (C3N4) has been widely used in the field of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, owning to its suitable band gap positions, high photochemical and thermal stability [1]. Nonetheless, pristine C3N4 synthesized by directly calcining precursors possess some limitations such as high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole and low specific surface area [2,3]. To overcome the defects of pristine C3N4, a variety of strategies have been widely explored, including morphology controlling [4], element doping [5], and heterojunction constructing [6]. In addition to the above strategies, the introduction of cocatalyst is another effective strategy for enhanced photocatalytic activity of C3N4 [7]. Noble metals (Pt, Ru and Pd, etc.) are commonly utilized as the excellent cocatalysts for improving photocatalytic hydrogen production of C3N4 due to the high work function and suitable H atom adsorption free energy [8–10]. However, the high-cost and scarcity of noble metals largely impede their large scale applications in the hydrogen production.

During the past decades, transition metal phosphides such as CoP [11], Ni2P5 [12] and Ni2P [13] were developed as reduced cocatalysts. Among them, the Ni2P is widely studied due to their simple preparation process and abundant raw material reserves [14]. Zeng et al. [15] and Wang et al. [16] verified that C3N4 nanosheets decorated with Ni2P nanoparticles show enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance of binary C3N4/Ni2P composite is still less than satisfactory due to the poor electrical conductivity of Ni2P [17].

Carbon dots, owning good electrical conductivity which facilitates the separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers, has attracted growing attention recently [18,19]. At present, CDs have been used in a variety of semiconductor materials, including Ag3PO4 [20], Cu2O [21], Fe2O3 [22], or ZnO [23], significantly improving the separation of photogenerated carriers. Based on above background, we anchored N-doped carbon dots and Ni2P nanoparticles onto C3N4 nanotube, respectively, to form CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite which will integrate respective advantages of NCDs and Ni2P nanoparticle. We further investigated the separation and transmission capabilities and mechanism of electron-hole in CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite. Systematical photocatalytic performance evaluation for CNT/NCDs, CNT/Ni2P and different doping proportions CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites were also carried out. The key finding is that CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites possess a double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism which can significantly inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole, and thus improve the hydrogen production rate. To the best of our knowledge, C3N4 nanotube/NCDs/Ni2P nanocomposite photocatalyst with double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism has not been reported yet in the open literature. Therefore, this work supplies new insights into designing and preparing photocatalyst with double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism.

Melamine, Citric acid, Urea, Ni(OAc)2·4H2O, NH4OH, NaH2PO2·H2O were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. The C3N4 nanotubes (CNT) and N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared according to the reported methods, respectively [24,25].

Preparation of CNT/NCDs nanocomposite: NCDs were anchored on the CNT during the CNT formation. In a typical experiment, melamine (5 g) were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water with ultrasonic treatment, then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hold at 200 °C for 12 h. After that, white solid intermediates were obtained after filtration and dry. Afterwards the intermediates were mixed with NCDs colloidal solution in proportion with stirring, then removed the solvent at 70 °C. The result mixture was heated for 4 h at 550 °C and CNT/NCDs nanocomposite were finally obtained.

Preparation of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite: CNT/NCDs/Ni(OH)2 composite was first synthesized by a hydrothermal method. In a detailed procedure, 100 mg of CNT/NCDs was dispersed into 0.625 mL of water and 30 mL of ethanol. The above compound was sonicated for 30 min. The different masses of Ni(OAc)2·4H2O and 0.625 mL of NH4OH were added respectively into above mixture, that was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and hold at 180 °C for 8 h. After that, the product was centrifuged and dried at 80 °C to obtain CNT/NCDs/Ni(OH)2 composite. 100 mg of CNT/NCDs/Ni(OH)2 composite and 50 mg of NaH2PO2·H2O were mingled and grinded. The above powder was calcinated at 300 °C in an Ar gas atmosphere with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 for 2 h. The obtained product was washed with ethanol, and then dried at 80 °C. The resultant product was denoted as CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-X (X = 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.0%, and 1.5%), where X is the weight percent of Ni2P nanoparticles.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was conducted to analyze the crystal structure of the sample by using XRD spectrometer. The morphology of the samples was recorded by a JEOL 2100 TEM microscope. Valence band XPS and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were examined on a Thermo Escalab 150 spectrometer with Al Kα X-rays. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was examined on a gas absorption analyzer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the samples were obtained using a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained on an Agilent UV–Vis spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were measured using a Nicolet Nexus 470 spectrometer. Electrochemical measurement was conducted on standard three-electrode system with catalyst as working electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and a piece of Pt sheet as counter electrode.

CEL-SPH2N photolysis water evolution system was used to investigate photocatalytic activities of sample. 50 mg photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of aqueous solution (10 mL triethanolamine and 90 mL distilled water). The light source was supplied by a 300 W Xe-lamp (Profect Light, PLS-SXE300C) equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter. Before the light irradiation, the suspension system was bubbled with nitrogen gas to remove air completely. The generated H2 was detected by a gas chromatographer (GC-7920).

The tubular structure could supply more exposed active edges, light reflection channels leading to long-distance and fast electron transmission, which were all beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic activity [30]. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted to study the morphology of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% as shown in Fig. 2 a–b. It is obvious that tubular morphology of C3N4 nanotube is well maintained after loading CDs and Ni2P, which is completely different from that of bulk C3N4 (Fig. S1). The TEM measurements further testified that the anticipative tubular morphology is successfully obtained in Fig. 2c–d. Fig. 2c showed the TEM image of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% sample. It clearly appear an unambiguous tubular structure. After loading NCDs and Ni2P, there is no obvious difference in nanostructure between CNT (Fig. S2) and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% (Fig. 2c), which was consistent with the SEM images. The HRTEM image of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% was displayed in Fig. 2d, revealing that the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm and 0.23 nm, which corresponded to the (111) plane of Ni2P and NCDs, respectively. Furthermore, the elemental distribution of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% was measured through elemental mapping images (Fig. S3), which illuminated the existence and distribution of C, N, Ni and P elements and confirmed that NCDs and Ni2P were evenly decorated on the C3N4 nanotube.

XPS spectra are recorded to gain deeper insight into the chemical states and surface species of C, N, Ni, and P for CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composite. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3 a) could be divided into three peaks at around 284.7, 286.1, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to graphite carbon atoms [31], the typical aromatic N-C-N coordination and the N-CN bond, respectively [32,33]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) at about 398.6, 399.8, 401.2 and 404.7 eV could be attributed to bicoordinated (N2C), sp3 (N-(C)3) groups, CNH functional groups and sp2 N atoms in triazine units, respectively [34,35]. As for Ni 2p (Fig. 3c), the peaks at 861.7 and 855.9 eV could be attribute to the Ni 2p3/2 peak and the oxidized Ni species (Ni2+), respectively [36], and the peaks at 880.6 and 873.9 eV were oxidized Ni species and the satellite of the Ni 2p1/2 peak, respectively [37]. With regard to the P 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d), the peak at 133.2 eV can be assigned to the surface nickel phosphate species [15].

Light absorption is the first step in photocatalytic decomposition of water and one of the important indexes to measure the photocatalytic capacity. The UV–vis spectra of pure C3N4 and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites with different Ni2P ratio was performed in Fig. 4 . The absorption of the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P samples gradually increased as the content of Ni2P increases because of the strong light absorption capacity of black-colored Ni2P [16].

Large specific surface area can provide more reaction sites, which is more conducive to promoting the enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. The specific surface area and corresponding pore size distribution curves of bulk C3N4 and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% were shown in Fig. 6 CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% exhibited a larger BET surface area of 29.7 m2 g−1 compared to that of bulk C3N4 (16.1 m2 g−1) (Fig. 6a). The high surface area of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% can be attributed to the unique 1D tubular structure. From Fig. 6b, it was found that the pore volume of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% was also increased.

Photocatalytic hydrogen performance of as-prepared photocatalysts was assessed by hydrogen production reaction under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm). Wherein triethanolamine (TEOA) was served as an h+ scavenger. Pure C3N4 and CNT/NCDs had almost no hydrogen production performance due to the rapid recombination of charge when no Ni2P exist. In the presence of 0.5% Ni2P, the photocatalytic performance of CNT/Ni2P-0.5% reached to 48.5 μmol g-1h−1. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% could reached to 627.2 μmol g-1h−1, which demonstrated that CDs played a vital role in the photocatalysis process. Therefore, 0.5% Ni2P was loaded on CNT/NCDs for photocatalytic hydrogen production performance test to explore the optimal content of NCDs (Fig. 7 a). When the amount of NCDs was increased to 1.0%, the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% shows the optimized H2 evolution rate of 627.2 μmol g-1h−1. Whereafter, continuing to increase the NCDs content lead to the reduced performance, which can be attributed to the shading effect of the excessive black NCDs.

Besides the influence of NCDs, the content of Ni2P also has a great influence on the photocatalytic performance of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites (Fig. 7b). When the Ni2P ratio in the composite attain to 0.5 wt%, the H2 production activity of the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites was significantly increased, reaching 627.17 μmol g-1h−1, which is approximately 13-fold than that of CNT/Ni2P-0.5%. The hydrogen evolution activity of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P gradually decreased with further increment in Ni2P loading. This phenomenon may be caused by two aspects. (i) Reaction sites are reduced due to overcrowding of Ni2P species on the surface of g-C3N4 [41]. (ii) Excess Ni2P covered on the CNT surface could inhibit the light absorption of CNT resulting to decrease the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity [42,43]. In summary, when the content of NCDs and Ni2P reached the optimal content of 1% and 0.5% respectively, the hydrogen production activity reached the optimal. As shown in Table S1, the H2 evolution rate of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P photocatalysts appeared to be higher than some the reported photocatalysts, which were tested in a wide range of conditions in the literature although light intensity, light source, sacrificial reagent and so on should be the same for a fairer comparison.

The cyclic stability of photocatalyst is the key index to evaluate its applicability. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there was no obvious decrease in H2 evolution after five cycles, which declared the excellent stability of the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composite. Moreover, the crystal structures and morphology of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composites were not changed as evidenced by the SEM (Fig. S4), XRD and FT-IR (Fig. S5), which further indicated that the photocatalyst was excellent stable under consecutive visible light irradiation.

PL measurement was conducted to assess the separation and transmission performance of charge carrier of sample. Fig. 8b presented the PL spectra of bulk C3N4, CNT, CNT/Ni2P-0.5% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%. The bulk C3N4 displayed a strong emission peak at 460 nm, which is derived from the high recombination of the photogenerated carriers [44]. When introducing NCDs and Ni2P, the PL intensities of CNT/Ni2P-0.5% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% were distinctly decreased, which originated from the rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons among C3N4, NCDs and Ni2P because of the tight heterojunction interface, resulting in the inhibition of photogenerated electron–hole recombination.

Photoelectrochemical(PEC) I-t curves and EIS Nyquist plots of samples were conducted to further reveal separation and recombination processes of photogenerated charges in semiconductors (Fig. 8c–d). it was obvious that CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% displayed a higher photocurrent density than pure C3N4 (Fig. 9 c), implying that efficient charge transfer was present in CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% and more photo-generated electron-hole were generated, whichkeptagreementwith the consequences of PL spectra shown in Fig. 8b. In the Nyquist plot (Fig. 8d), the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composite exhibited the smallest arc radius compared with bulk C3N4 and CNT/Ni2P-0.5%, demonstrating that the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% has the lowest charge transfer resistance. Z-view software was employed to simulate the randles electrical equivalent-circuit model (inset of Fig. 8d), in which the Rs represents the solution resistance and Rp stands for electron-transfer resistance [45]. These consequences further testify that the presence of NCDs and Ni2P could improve separation and transfer efficiencies of electron-hole pairs. CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5%, CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.0% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.5% displayed almost identical band gap as shown in Fig. S6. The CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.5% composite exhibited the smallest arc radius compared with CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% and CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-1.0% (Fig. S7b). However, the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% composite exhibits the highest photocurrent density (Fig. S7a), which is match well with the H2 production performance of the CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites. This results suggest that photogenerated electron-hole separation may play a major role in this material.

The band alignment among CNT, NCDs and Ni2P leading to the formation of double Z-scheme structure, which plays a key role in optimizing the photocatalytic activity of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P. The plots of (αhν)1/2 versus (hν) of bulk C3N4 and CNT was showed in Fig. 9a. CNT displayed a shrinking band gap of 2.60 eV compared with bulk C3N4 (2.65 eV). This phenomenon could be attributed to the quantum confinement effect of CNT [46]. Mott-Schottky plots was used to explore the flat band potentials of CNT. The positive slope of the linellae indicated n-type characteristic of C3N4. The flat band potential for CNT were −1.08 V (vs Ag/AgCl, pH = 7) originated from intercept to the x-axis of Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 9b).

On the basis of the above experimental consequences of photocatalytic H2 evolution, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 10 . According to the result of UV–vis spectra, the band gap energies of the C3N4 nanotube was 2.60 eV. The band gap and flat band potential of Ni2P was 0.85 eV and −1.60 V (vs Ag/AgCl, pH = 7) (Fig. S8). The CB position ECB(Ag/AgCl) is more negative about 0.1–0.3 V than the flat band potential retrieved from Mott-Schottky analysis [47]. The CB position ECB is −1.28 V (vs Ag/AgCl, pH = 7). Therefore, the CB position (ECB) of the samples were calculated to be −1.08 V vs NHE at pH = 7 after conversion using the equation: ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 V [48]. Calculated by the empirical Eqs: EVB = ECB + Eg [49], the C3N4 nantube had a valence band potential of 1.52 V, while the VB and CB potentials of Ni2P are about −0.75 and −1.60 V, respectively. According to our previous work, the VB and CB potentials of NCDs are about 1.87 and 0.57 V, respectively [3]. When CNT, NCDs and Ni2P in the composite material absorb photons of certain energy under light irradiation, electrons are excited from their respective valence band to conduction band. Due to the ample thermodynamic driving force, the electrons in the conduction band of C3N4 can migrate to the valence band of Ni2P. Meanwhile, the electrons can jump from the conduction band of NCDs to the valence band of C3N4, resulting in the photogenerated electrons-holes could eventually accumulate in the CB of Ni2P and VB of NCDs in CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composites, respectively. Therefore, CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite material can not only maintain the high oxidation capacity of NCDs, but also maintain the high reduction capacity of Ni2P, supplying a larger driving force for photocatalytic hydrogen production reaction. This charge transfer process in the double Z-scheme greatly improves the efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole separation, resulting in the enhancement of the photocatalytic water splitting performance.

To sum up, a novel ternary C3N4 nanotubes/NCDs/Ni2P hybrid has been successfully designed via two-step hydrothermal and phosphidation method. In comparision with CNT/NCDs and CNT/Ni2P, respectively, ternary CNT/NCDs/Ni2P-0.5% displayed enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of 627.2 µmol g-1h−1, which was 13 times higher than that of CNT/Ni2P-0.5 wt%. The obvious improvement in photocatalytic activity of CNT/NCDs/Ni2P composite was predominantly ascribed to the tubular structure and the formation of double Z-scheme heterojunction among C3N4 nanotubes, NCDs and Ni2P, which availably inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole and supplied sufficient active sites for hydrogen evolution reaction. Meanwhile, the synthesized C3N4 nanotube/NCDs/Ni2P possess a impressive highly cycle stability. This work supplies new insights into designing and preparing photocatalyst with double Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism.

Yingying Jiao: Conceptualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yike Li: Methodology, Validation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Jianshe Wang: Writing - review & editing. Zhanhang He: Writing - review & editing. Zhongjun Li: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21671176 and 21001096).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147603.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic C3N4 based noble-metal-free photocatalyst systems: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Che, W. Cheng, T. Yao, F. Tang, W. Liu, H. Su, Y. Huang, Q. Liu, J. Liu, F. Hu, Z. Pan, Z. Sun, S. Wei, Fast Photoelectron Transfer in (Cring)-C3N4 Plane Heterostructural Nanosheets for Overall Water Splitting, J Am Chem Soc, 139 (2017) 3021-3026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated Z-scheme g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots heterostructure photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jiao, Q. Huang, J. Wang, Z. He, Z. Li, A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated Z-scheme g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots heterostructure photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 247 (2019) 124-132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Half-metallic carbon nitride nanosheets with micro grid mode resonance structure for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zhou, Y. Shan, Y. Hu, X. Xu, L. Long, J. Zhang, J. Dai, J. Guo, J. Shen, S. Li, L. Liu, X. Wu, Half-metallic carbon nitride nanosheets with micro grid mode resonance structure for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, Nat Commun, 9 (2018) 3366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doping of graphitic carbon nitride for photocatalysis: A reveiw

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of an all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst based on graphite carbon nitride and its enhancement to catalytic activity

S. Cao, J. Yu, g-C3N4-Based Photocatalysts for Hydrogen Generation, J Phys Chem Lett, 5 (2014) 2101-2107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic activities of graphitic carbon nitride powder for water reduction and oxidation under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Maeda, X. Wang, Y. Nishihara, Photocatalytic Activities of Graphitic Carbon Nitride Powder for Water Reduction and Oxidation under Visible Light, J.phys.chem.c, 113 (2009) 4940-4947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient photocatalytic H? Evolution from water using visible light and structure-controlled graphitic carbon nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. David James, Q. Kaipei, S. Stephen Andrew, H. Albertus Denny, C. Xiaowei, G. Zhengxiao, T. Junwang, Highly efficient photocatalytic H? evolution from water using visible light and structure-controlled graphitic carbon nitride, Angew Chem Int Ed Engl, 53 (2015) 9240-9245.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Making metal-carbon nitride heterojunctions for improved photocatalytic hydrogen evolution with visible light
